subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermochemistry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
3. Answer the following questions about an experiment in which CaCO (s) is combined with HCl(aq), represented
by the following balanced equation.
CaCO (s) + 2HCl(aq) → CaCl (aq) + CO (g) + $H_2O$(l)
3 2 2 2

parts.prompt:
Write the balanced net ionic equation for the reaction.
A student performs an investigation to study factors that affect the rate of the reaction. In each trial the student
combines 50.0 mL of HCl(aq) at 21.2° C with 1.00 g of CaCO (s) and measures the time required for the
reaction to go to completion. The data are given in the following table.
Concentration of Particle Size of Time of Reaction
Trial
HCl(aq) (M ) CaCO (s) (s)
1 1.00 Fine powder 67
2 1.00 Small chunks 112
31 .00 Large chunk 342
4 3.00 Fine powder 22
53 .00 Small chunks 227
63 .00 Large chunk 114

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced equation (state symbols not required): 1 point

required_elements: Provide a correct response from the accepted alternatives
alternatives.correct_example: 2 3 22 CaCO ( ) 2 H ( ) Ca ( ) CO ( ) H O( ) s aq aq g l+++ → ++

parts.prompt:
The student correctly identifies that trial 5 is inconsistent with the other trials. Explain why the student’s claim
is correct using the data in the table.

required_elements: Provide a correct response from the accepted alternatives
alternatives.correct_example: Even though the concentration of HCl is greater in trial 5 than in trial 2, the reaction time is significantly longer. Both trial 2 and 5 occur under otherwise identical conditions. The trend for trial 1 and 4 indicates that the higher concentration of HCl results in a shorter time of reaction

parts.prompt:
Based on the reaction conditions and the collisions that occur between particles, explain the reason for the
difference in the reaction times for trial 2 and trial 3.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of the effect of surface area on reaction time: 1 point

required_elements: For a correct explanation of the effect of surface area on reaction time: 1 point
alternatives.correct_example: The time of reaction in trial 2 is shorter than that in trial 3 because the calcium carbonate

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation of the effect of particle collisions on reaction rate: 1 point

required_elements: For a correct explanation of the effect of particle collisions on reaction rate: 1 point
alternatives.correct_example: The larger interface between the two reacting substances means there will be more

parts.prompt:
The student claims that the reaction is zero order with respect to HCl(aq). Do you agree or disagree with the
student’s claim? Justify your answer using the student’s data.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point

required_elements: Provide a correct response from the accepted alternatives
alternatives.correct_example: Disagree. If the reaction was zeroth order with respect to HCl, then changing the concentration of HCl would not affect the rate of reaction, and the time of reaction would be the same for trials in which the only difference was [HCl] . The student’s data for trial s 1 and 4 ( likewise f or 3 and 6) show that changing [HCl] significantly alters the time of reaction

parts.prompt:
The HCl(aq) was present in excess in all trials of the experiment. Determine the molarity of the HCl(aq) in
the beaker after the reaction is complete in trial 2. Assume that the volume of the mixture remains constant at
50.0 mL throughout the trial. (The molar mass of CaCO is 100.09 g/mol.)

CaCO (s) + 2HCl(aq) → CaCl (aq) + CO (g) + $H_2O$(l)
3 2 2 2
In order to measure the enthalpy of the reaction shown, the student repeats trial 1 by mixing 50.0 mL of HCl(aq)
with 1.00 g of CaCO (s) using a coffee cup calorimeter. The student records the temperature of the system every
20 seconds. The data are given in the following table.
Measured Temperature of
Time (s)
Solution (° C)
0 21.20
20 21.51
40 21.70
60 21.85
80 21.90
100 21.90

parts.prompt:
Based on the experimental data, the mass of the system is 51.0 g, and the specific heat of the reaction mixture
is 4.0 J / (g · ° C).
(i) Calculate the magnitude of heat transfer, q, in joules.
(ii) Calculate the enthalpy of reaction in units of kJ/mol . Include the algebraic sign on your answer.
rxn
